original_abstract:
Secondary organic aerosol (SOA) formation from the oxidation of $\alpha$-pinene by both ozone (O$_3$) and hydroxyl radicals (OH) was investigated using a dual outdoor smog chamber. The study focused on the chemical characterization of carboxylic acids and high-molecular-weight dimers as a function of relative humidity (RH), aerosol acidity, and oxidant type. Under high-NO$_x$ conditions, O$_3$-initiated oxidation produced substantially higher SOA yields than OH-initiated oxidation, though RH had a negligible effect on total SOA mass. Chemical analysis via UPLC/ESI-HR-Q-TOFMS identified eight carboxylic acids and four distinct dimers (MW 344, 358, 368, and 388). 

A key finding was that dimers formed exclusively during $\alpha$-pinene ozonolysis and were entirely absent in OH-initiated experiments, even when monomer precursors were present. Dimer concentrations doubled at high RH (50–90%) compared to lower RH (30–50%), a trend that correlated with enhanced new particle formation through homogeneous nucleation. In contrast, aerosol acidity did not significantly influence dimer yields. Highly oxidized species such as 3-methyl-1,2,3-butanetricarboxylic acid (MBTCA) and diaterpenylic acid acetate (DTAA) were more characteristic of OH-radical aging. The results suggest that these dimers are formed via gas-phase reactions involving stabilized Criegee intermediates (sCIs) rather than through previously hypothesized particle-phase esterification. Given their presence in both laboratory and ambient samples, these high-molecular-weight dimers serve as specific tracers for $\alpha$-pinene ozonolysis and can provide insights into the role of anthropogenic pollutants (like O$_3$) in biogenic aerosol formation.
humanized_abstract:
Secondary organic aerosol (SOA) formed from the oxidation of α-pinene by both ozone (O3) and hydroxyl radicals (OH) were studied using a dual outdoor smog chamber facility. The objective of this study was to investigate the composition of carboxylic acids and high-MW dimers as a function of relative humidity (RH), aerosol acidity and whether the SOA was formed with ozone or hydroxyl radicals. Under high-NOx conditions O3- induced SOA yields were found to be several times larger than those induced by OH. Under these conditions changes in RH had little impact on the overall mass of the SOA. Ultrahigh performance liquid chromatography coupled with electrospray ionization high resolution quadrupole time of flight mass spectrometry (UPLC/ESI-HR-Q-TOFMS) analysis identified eight carboxylic acids and four different dimers with molecular weights of 344, 358, 368 and 388.

We report detailed studies of the production of high-molecular-weight dimers derived from $\alpha$-pinene via ozonolysis and OH initiation under high and low RH and their impact on secondary organic aerosol (SOA) acidification. Notably, dimers formed only during ozonolysis and were absent during OH initiation of SOA from $\alpha$-pinene or its precursors across a range of RH from 30 to 90%. Their production was enhanced under conditions of high RH where they contributed to a doubling in the yields of homogeneously nucleated new particles. Aerosol acidification by these dimers, while measured to vary over only a factor of approximately 2 over the entire range of conditions, is generally modest. High levels of highly oxidized compounds such as MBTCA and DTAA were also formed through OH radical aging in the SOA from $\alpha$-pinene and other monoterpenes. The dimers are produced from sCIs that react in the gas phase rather than in the particle phase through an esterification process as previously inferred. Their presence in situ production is established unequivocally through analysis of laboratory samples, and their established presence in ambient aerosols suggest a method for tracing $\alpha$-pinene ozonolysis and thereby assessing the potential impact of O3 and other anthropogenic aerosol acidification.

document.output:

document.input:
Secondary organic aerosol (SOA) formation from the oxidation of $\alpha$-pinene by both ozone (O$_3$) and hydroxyl radicals (OH) was investigated using a dual outdoor smog chamber. Given their presence in both laboratory and ambient samples, these high-molecular-weight dimers serve as specific tracers for $\alpha$-pinene ozonolysis and can provide insights into the role of anthropogenic pollutants (like O$_3$) in biogenic aerosol formation.
